section.title: Malta and the Arrival in Rome
section.sectionRationale: The book reaches its goal. After Malta, where a viper cannot harm Paul and God heals through him, the company finally comes to Rome. There Paul reasons with the Jewish leaders, some believe and some do not, and Luke lets the ancient warning of Isaiah frame the turn to the Gentiles. Acts closes not with Paul's death but with the gospel preached boldly and unhindered in the capital of the world, its story deliberately left open.
section.thesis: Preserved through snakebite and sea, Paul comes at last to Rome, where he lays the risen Christ before Israel from Moses and the Prophets; and though some will not believe, God's salvation goes to the Gentiles, and the word is proclaimed in the heart of the empire boldly and without hindrance.
section.frameNote: Five movements: Paul unharmed by the viper on Malta; the healings that follow; the journey on to Rome and the welcome of the brothers; Paul's reasoning with the Roman Jews; and Isaiah's warning, the turn to the Gentiles, and the open ending of the gospel preached unhindered.

units.label: Ashore on Malta
units.note: the viper that could not harm him

verses.text: Once we were safely ashore, we learned that the island was called Malta.
The islanders showed us extraordinary kindness. They kindled a fire and welcomed all of us because it was raining and cold.
Paul gathered a bundle of sticks, and as he laid them on the fire, a viper, driven out by the heat, fastened itself to his hand.
When the islanders saw the creature hanging from his hand, they said to one another, “Surely this man is a murderer. Although he was saved from the sea, Justice has not allowed him to live.”
But Paul shook the creature off into the fire and suffered no ill effects.
The islanders were expecting him to swell up or suddenly drop dead. But after waiting a long time and seeing nothing unusual happen to him, they changed their minds and said he was a god.
Nearby stood an estate belonging to Publius, the chief official of the island. He welcomed us and entertained us hospitably for three days.
The father of Publius was sick in bed, suffering from fever and dysentery. Paul went in to see him, and after praying and placing his hands on him, he healed the man.
After this had happened, the rest of the sick on the island came and were cured as well.
The islanders honored us in many ways and supplied our needs when we were ready to sail.

units.label: The road to Rome
units.note: he was encouraged and gave thanks to God

verses.text: After three months we set sail in an Alexandrian ship that had wintered in the island. It had the Twin Brothers as a figurehead.
Putting in at Syracuse, we stayed there three days.
From there we weighed anchor and came to Rhegium. After one day, a south wind came up, and on the second day we arrived at Puteoli.
There we found some brothers who invited us to spend the week with them. And so we came to Rome.
The brothers there had heard about us and traveled as far as the Forum of Appius and the Three Taverns to meet us. When Paul saw them, he was encouraged and gave thanks to God.
When we arrived in Rome, Paul was permitted to stay by himself, with a soldier to guard him.

units.label: Paul reasons with the Jews of Rome
units.note: the kingdom of God, and Jesus from Moses and the Prophets

verses.text: After three days, he called together the leaders of the Jews. When they had gathered, he said to them, “Brothers, although I have done nothing against our people or the customs of our fathers, I was taken prisoner in Jerusalem and handed over to the Romans.
They examined me and wanted to release me, because there was no basis for a death sentence against me.
But when the Jews objected, I was compelled to appeal to Caesar, even though I have no charge to bring against my nation.
So for this reason I have called to see you and speak with you. It is because of the hope of Israel that I am bound with this chain.”
The leaders replied, “We have not received any letters about you from Judea, nor have any of the brothers from there reported or even mentioned anything bad about you.
But we consider your views worth hearing, because we know that people everywhere are speaking against this sect.”

So they set a day to meet with Paul, and many people came to the place he was staying. He expounded to them from morning to evening, testifying about the {{basileia|kingdom}} of God and persuading them about Jesus from the Law of Moses and the Prophets.
Some of them were convinced by what he said, but others refused to believe.

units.label: Isaiah's warning and the open ending
units.note: God's salvation sent to the Gentiles, proclaimed unhindered

verses.text: They disagreed among themselves and began to leave after Paul had made this final statement: “The Holy Spirit was right when He spoke to your fathers through Isaiah the prophet:
‘Go to this people and say, “You will be ever hearing but never understanding; you will be ever seeing but never perceiving.”

For this people’s heart has grown callous; they hardly hear with their ears, and they have closed their eyes. Otherwise they might see with their eyes, hear with their ears, understand with their hearts, and turn, and I would heal them.’
Be advised, therefore, that God’s {{soterion|salvation}} has been sent to the Gentiles, and they will listen!”
Paul stayed there two full years in his own rented house, welcoming all who came to visit him.
{{parrhesia|Boldly}} and {{akolytos|freely}} he proclaimed the kingdom of God and taught about the Lord Jesus Christ.

basileia.gloss: Kingdom, kingly rule, reign. Acts opens and closes with the kingdom of God, Acts 1:3 and 28:31. Paul testifies to God's reign and persuades people about Jesus from the Scriptures, for the kingdom has come near in the crucified and risen King, and the gospel of the kingdom is now announced in Rome itself.

soterion.gloss: Salvation, God's saving deliverance. Paul declares that this salvation has been sent to the Gentiles, and they will listen. The word gathers up the whole aim of Acts, the good news of rescue in Christ, now going out beyond Israel to the nations, exactly as the prophets foretold.

parrhesia.gloss: Boldness, frank and open speech, freedom to say all. It is a keynote of Acts: the Spirit-filled church speaks the word with boldness. The book ends with Paul proclaiming Christ with this same parrhesia, undimmed by chains, a fitting close to the story of a gospel that will not be silenced.

akolytos.gloss: Without hindrance, unhindered. This is the very last word of Acts in the Greek. Though Paul is a prisoner awaiting Caesar, the word of God runs free. Luke ends not with a period on Paul's story but with the gospel advancing openly and unstoppably in the heart of the empire.

basic.context: The shipwrecked company lands on Malta, where a deadly viper fails to harm Paul and God heals many through him. After three months they sail on and finally reach Rome. There Paul, though a prisoner, gathers the Jewish leaders, explains his case, and spends a long day laying out the kingdom of God and Jesus from the Scriptures. Some believe, some do not, and Paul announces that salvation is going to the Gentiles. The book ends with him preaching freely for two years.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the ending as the deliberate climax of the whole book. Watch the Malta scene show God's protection and mercy, then the arrival fulfill the long promise of reaching Rome. The Isaiah quotation and the turn to the Gentiles gather up a theme that runs through all of Luke and Acts, and the final two verses, open and unfinished, are the point: the gospel goes on.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul testifies to the basileia, the kingdom, of God and persuades people about Jesus. He announces that God's soterion, salvation, has gone to the Gentiles. And the book ends with him preaching with parrhesia, boldness, and akolytos, without hindrance, the very last word of Acts.
basic.typology: The viper that cannot harm Paul recalls the promise that the seed of the woman would crush the serpent, and that God's servants would tread on serpents unharmed. Paul, bearing the gospel safely through venom and storm, pictures the church carrying Christ's victory over the ancient serpent to the nations.
basic.christ: Everything centers on Jesus. Paul preaches the kingdom of God and persuades people about Jesus from Moses and the Prophets, then teaches about the Lord Jesus Christ to all who come. The salvation sent to the Gentiles is salvation in Him. The risen and reigning Christ is the content of the message that fills the empire's capital.
basic.god: God keeps every promise. He preserved Paul through the sea, the snake, and the long road, and brought him to Rome to testify, just as He said. God's salvation, long promised to the nations, now goes out to the Gentiles, and His word cannot be bound even when His servant is. The whole story is the outworking of God's sovereign, saving purpose.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 1:8 set the program, witnesses to the ends of the earth. Isaiah 6:9 to 10 is the warning Paul quotes over unbelief. Luke 24:47 promised repentance preached to all nations. Romans, written earlier to this very city, unfolds the gospel Paul now preaches in Rome.
basic.oneStory: From Jerusalem to Rome, the risen Christ has poured out His Spirit and driven the gospel to the ends of the earth. The book ends with the word preached freely in the heart of the empire, its story left open because the mission is not finished; it continues through the church to this day.

context.literary: This is the intended conclusion of Acts, and its abrupt, open ending is a masterstroke. Luke does not record Paul's trial or death; he leaves the gospel being preached unhindered. The Isaiah citation forms a bookend with the theme of Israel's mixed response throughout Luke and Acts, and the final adverb, unhindered, sums up the triumph of the word over every obstacle.
context.historical: Malta lay in the central Mediterranean; Publius was its leading official. Puteoli was a major port near Naples, and the Forum of Appius and Three Taverns were stops on the Appian Way toward Rome. Paul's custody, a house arrest with a guarding soldier, allowed visitors and preaching. The two years likely fall around AD 60 to 62, before the outcome of his appeal.
context.canonical: The ending fulfills the risen Lord's commission that the gospel reach the ends of the earth, Acts 1:8, with Rome standing for the whole Gentile world. The Isaiah quotation ties the book to the same text Jesus used of unbelieving hearers, Luke 8:10, and the turn to the Gentiles echoes Simeon's song that Christ is a light for the nations, Luke 2:30 to 32.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bring the gospel from Jerusalem to Rome as promised, to account for Israel's divided response by Isaiah's ancient word, to confirm the mission to the Gentiles, and to leave the story open so that the church continues the unhindered advance of the word.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes how the gospel reached Rome and how Paul was received; it does not prescribe every detail as a pattern. The abiding truths are God's faithfulness to His promises, the mixed reception of the gospel, the mission to the nations, and the unstoppable progress of the word.

debates.question: Why does Acts end so abruptly, without telling us what happened to Paul?

views.label: The book's true subject is the gospel, not Paul
views.case: Luke set out to show the word of God advancing from Jerusalem to the ends of the earth, Acts 1:8; reaching Rome with the message preached boldly and unhindered completes that aim, so the story ends on the triumph of the word rather than the fate of one man, and the final word, unhindered, is the deliberate climax.

views.label: Luke wrote before the outcome was known
views.case: Others hold that Luke simply finished his account around AD 62, before Paul's trial was decided, so the ending reflects the state of things at the time of writing; on this view the abruptness is historical circumstance more than literary design, though it still leaves the gospel visibly advancing.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the focus falls on the unstoppable progress of the word, not on closure for Paul. The open ending invites every later reader to see the mission as unfinished and to take up the same task; whether by design or circumstance, Acts leaves the gospel running free and hands the story on to the church.

study.originalLanguages: The close of Acts is carried by a few charged words. Paul testifies to the basileia, the kingdom, of God, the reign of God that has drawn near in the crucified and risen King. He announces that God's soterion, salvation, has been sent to the Gentiles, the aim of the whole book now realized. And Luke ends by saying Paul preached with parrhesia, boldness, and akolytos, without hindrance, the very last word in the Greek. Though the apostle is chained, the word of God is not, and the story is left running.

typology.type: The serpent that cannot harm God's servant
typology.fulfillment: The harmless viper recalls the promise that the woman's seed would crush the serpent and that His people would tread on serpents unharmed, Genesis 3:15 and Luke 10:19, fulfilled in Christ's victory carried by His church.
The salvation sent to the nations fulfills Isaiah's servant and Simeon's song, a light for revelation to the Gentiles, Isaiah 49:6 and Luke 2:32, now realized as the gospel fills Rome.
The reign of God announced from Moses and the Prophets is fulfilled in Jesus the King, whom Paul preaches openly in the capital, the gospel of the kingdom for all peoples, Luke 24:44 to 47.

study.christ: Christ is the substance of the ending. Paul spends whole days persuading people about Jesus from the Law of Moses and the Prophets, and the final verse says he taught about the Lord Jesus Christ boldly and unhindered. The kingdom of God he proclaims is the reign of the risen King. The salvation sent to the Gentiles is salvation found in Him alone. Acts closes as it began, with the exalted Christ at the center, His word going out to the ends of the earth.
study.god: God is faithful and sovereign to the last line. He guards Paul through venom, sickness, sea, and imprisonment, and brings him to Rome exactly as promised. He sends His salvation to the Gentiles according to His ancient word, and He keeps His gospel free even when His servant is bound. The unhindered preaching that ends the book is the triumph of God's saving purpose, which no power on earth can stop.

crossReferences.note: The commission to be witnesses to the ends of the earth, fulfilled as the gospel reaches Rome.
The prophecy of hardened hearing Paul applies to the unbelief he meets, as Jesus also applied it.
Simeon's song that Christ is God's salvation and a light for the Gentiles, now going out to the nations.
The gospel Paul preaches in Rome, the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes, Jew first and also Greek.

study.oneStory: Acts ends where the whole Bible is heading: the reign of God announced to all nations through the risen Christ. The gospel has traveled from Jerusalem to Rome, and though some in Israel will not believe, salvation goes to the Gentiles and the word is preached boldly and without hindrance. The story is left open on purpose, for the mission continues, and the exalted Christ is still gathering His people from the ends of the earth.

quiz.q: What is the significance of the open, unfinished ending of Acts?

options.text: The focus is the unstoppable advance of the gospel, left running so the church continues the mission.
options.note: Luke ends on the word preached boldly and unhindered, the true subject of the book, not Paul's fate.

options.text: Luke may have written before the outcome of Paul's trial was known.
options.note: A reasonable historical explanation, but it still leaves the emphasis on the gospel's ongoing progress rather than closure for Paul.

options.text: Luke ran out of information about Paul's later life.
options.note: This underestimates the deliberate design; the ending fits Luke's stated aim of the word reaching the ends of the earth.

options.text: The ending shows the mission had failed in Rome.
options.note: The opposite; the gospel is preached freely and openly, a note of triumph, not failure.

quiz.q: How did the islanders of Malta interpret the viper on Paul's hand?

options.text: First that he was a murderer being punished, then that he was a god when he was unharmed.
options.note: They swung from one superstition to the other, verses 4 and 6, while God simply preserved His servant.

options.text: That he was a murderer whom Justice would not let live.
options.note: True of their first reaction, but they later reversed and called him a god.

options.text: That the bite was an accident of no meaning.
options.note: They read deep meaning into it, first condemnation, then divinity; they did not treat it as meaningless.

options.text: That Paul had tamed the snake by magic.
options.note: They said nothing about magic; they concluded he must be a god.

quiz.q: How did the Jewish leaders in Rome respond to Paul's teaching about Jesus?

options.text: Some were convinced, but others refused to believe, and they left divided.
options.note: Luke records a split response, verses 24 to 25, the mixed reception that runs through Acts.

options.text: They all rejected his message and drove him away.
options.note: Not all rejected it; some were convinced, though the group left in disagreement.

options.text: They all believed and were baptized.
options.note: The response was divided, not a wholesale conversion.

options.text: They reported him to Caesar as a troublemaker.
options.note: Nothing of the kind; they had received no charges against him and came to hear his views.

quiz.q: What does Paul announce after quoting Isaiah's warning?

options.text: That God's salvation has been sent to the Gentiles, and they will listen.
options.note: Paul declares the turn to the Gentiles, verse 28, a key theme of the whole book.

options.text: That the hearts of the people have grown callous.
options.note: That is Isaiah's warning he quotes, verse 27, but his announcement that follows is the sending of salvation to the Gentiles.

options.text: That Israel has been permanently cast off by God.
options.note: Paul does not say Israel is cast off; some believed, and elsewhere he holds out hope for his people.

options.text: That he will stop preaching to everyone.
options.note: The opposite; he keeps preaching boldly for two more years to all who come.

quiz.q: How does the book of Acts describe Paul's preaching at its very end?

options.text: He proclaimed the kingdom of God and taught about the Lord Jesus Christ boldly and without hindrance.
options.note: The final verse ends on the word unhindered, the triumphant close of the book, verse 31.

options.text: He welcomed all who came to visit him in his rented house.
options.note: True, verse 30, but the climactic note is the bold, unhindered preaching of Christ.

options.text: He preached quietly and in secret to avoid trouble.
options.note: The opposite; he spoke openly and boldly, without hindrance.

options.text: He gave up preaching while awaiting his trial.
options.note: He did not give up; he proclaimed Christ freely throughout the two years.

questions: Acts ends with the gospel still on the move and the story left open. How does it change your sense of purpose to know you are part of a mission that is not yet finished?
Paul was chained, yet the word of God was not hindered. Where do your own limits or circumstances tempt you to think the gospel is stuck, when God can still work freely?
The seaside islanders showed extraordinary kindness, and Paul repaid it with healing and witness. How might ordinary hospitality, given or received, open a door for the gospel?
When Paul saw the Roman brothers on the road, he was encouraged and gave thanks to God. Who has God placed in your life to strengthen you, and have you thanked Him for them?
Paul kept preaching Christ boldly through two uncertain years. Where is God calling you to steady, faithful witness even when the outcome is unclear?

translationNotes.note: The BSB omits verse 29, following the earliest and best Greek manuscripts, which move from verse 28 to verse 30. A footnote records that the later Byzantine text and Received Text add: When he had said this, the Jews went away, disputing sharply among themselves. Because the sentence is absent from the oldest manuscripts, the verse number is left unused. We carry the omission faithfully.

Paul quotes Isaiah 6:9 to 10, the same prophecy Jesus applied to hardened hearers. It explains Israel's divided response without denying God's continuing purpose, and it frames the sending of salvation to the Gentiles.

The final phrase renders two Greek terms: with parrhesia, boldness or openness, and akolytos, without hindrance, which is the very last word of the book. Luke ends deliberately on the unstoppable freedom of the gospel, even while Paul remains a prisoner.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 28. Verse 28:29 is omitted by the BSB per the earliest manuscripts and is carried as an omission with a translation note. The poetry of verses 26 to 27 was built from the text elements only, joined with spaces; footnote markers, line breaks, and poem or speaker markers were skipped. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
